messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following biometric access control system notice for GDPR compliance:

"This building uses fingerprint scanning for access control. Your fingerprint is converted to a numerical template and the original image is not stored. Templates are used solely to verify your identity at entry points. Enrolment is mandatory for all employees. Templates are deleted when employment ends. Contact facilities@company.com for queries."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] No lawful basis stated. Mandatory enrolment in an employment context cannot rely on consent (not freely given). Likely legitimate interest or legal obligation, but neither is named.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [FAIL] Fingerprint templates constitute biometric data used for unique identification under Art. 9(1). No Art. 9(2) basis is stated. Consent is explicitly invalid as enrolment is mandatory. A specific Art. 9(2) basis under national law must be identified.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Identity verification for building access is a clear and specific purpose.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Fingerprint-to-template conversion without storing the original image is a positive minimisation measure. Whether the template can be reverse-engineered is not addressed.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Deletion upon employment termination is a defined event-based retention limit.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] No data subject rights stated beyond a generic contact email.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] The biometric access control system provider is a processor and is not disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not mentioned. Systematic biometric processing of employees requires a DPIA (Art. 35) and likely a DPO.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (no lawful basis), Art. 9 (biometric data without valid Art. 9(2) basis — consent invalid as enrolment is mandatory), Art. 15-21 (rights absent), Art. 28 (system provider not disclosed), Art. 35 (DPIA required for systematic biometric processing), Art. 37 (DPO likely required), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Identify a valid Art. 9(2) basis under applicable national employment law (cannot be consent if mandatory)
- State the Art. 6 lawful basis explicitly
- Conduct and reference a DPIA for biometric access control
- Add full data subject rights section
- Disclose the access control system provider as a processor
- Address international transfer safeguards
- Appoint DPO and add breach notification commitment
- Evaluate whether less intrusive alternatives were considered as a proportionality measure
